title: The Word, John's witness, and first disciples
summary_text: The chapter opens with the Word’s identity with God, creation through him, life and light for humanity, and John’s witness to the coming light. John denies being the Christ, Elijah, or the prophet, points to one greater after him, and calls Jesus the Lamb of God and Son of God after the Spirit’s descent. Andrew, Simon (named Peter), Philip, and Nathanael are drawn to Jesus, who shows knowledge of Nathanael and promises they will see greater things.
key_points: The Word was with God and was God; all things were made through him.
John was sent to testify about the light so people might believe.
Those who received Jesus were given the right to become God’s children by believing in his name.
The Word became flesh; John identified Jesus as the Lamb of God and testified he is the Son of God.
Andrew brought Simon; Jesus renamed him Cephas (Peter).
Philip brought Nathanael; Jesus revealed knowledge of him under the fig tree.
Jesus said they would see heaven opened and angels on the Son of Man.
evidence_phrases: In the beginning was the Word
All things were made through him
The true light that enlightens everyone
to them he gave the right to become God’s children
The Word became flesh
Behold, the Lamb of God
this is the Son of God
You shall be called Cephas
when you were under the fig tree, I saw you
the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man

title: Sign at Cana and cleansing the temple
summary_text: At a wedding in Cana, Jesus turns water into wine when his mother tells him there is no wine; the ruler of the feast praises the bridegroom, and the disciples believe as Jesus reveals his glory. Jesus then goes to Jerusalem for Passover, drives merchants from the temple, speaks of raising the temple in three days (meaning his body), and many believe while he does not entrust himself to them because he knows what is in people.
key_points: Jesus’s mother told him the wine had run out; he had servants fill jars and the water became wine.
The disciples believed because this was the beginning of his signs and he revealed his glory.
Jesus drove sellers and money changers from the temple and said not to make his Father’s house a marketplace.
Jesus said destroy this temple and in three days he would raise it up, speaking of the temple of his body.
After he was raised, the disciples remembered and believed the Scripture and his word.
Many believed at Passover when they saw his signs, but Jesus did not entrust himself to them.
Jesus knew what was in everyone without needing testimony.
evidence_phrases: They have no wine
Fill the water pots with water
You have kept the good wine until now
This beginning of his signs
Don’t make my Father’s house a marketplace
Zeal for your house will eat me up
Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up
he spoke of the temple of his body
Jesus didn’t entrust himself to them
he himself knew what was in man

title: Born anew, God’s Son, and John’s joy
summary_text: Nicodemus comes by night; Jesus teaches that one must be born anew of water and Spirit to enter God’s Kingdom and compares the Spirit to the wind. Jesus speaks of the Son of Man lifted up like Moses’s serpent, God’s love and sending of the Son for eternal life, and light versus darkness. Jesus then baptizes in Judea while John baptizes near Salim; John’s disciples question Jesus’s popularity, and John insists he must decrease while the one from heaven must increase, ending with belief in the Son and warning of God’s wrath on the disobedient.
key_points: Nicodemus said no one could do Jesus’s signs unless God were with him.
Jesus said one must be born anew to see God’s Kingdom and be born of water and Spirit to enter it.
Jesus compared the Spirit’s working to the wind’s unseen coming and going.
The Son of Man must be lifted up so believers may have eternal life.
God loved the world and gave his one and only Son so believers would not perish.
People loved darkness rather than light because their works were evil.
A dispute arose between John’s disciples and some Jews about purification.
John said he must decrease and Christ must increase; belief in the Son brings eternal life.
evidence_phrases: unless one is born anew
born of water and spirit
So is everyone who is born of the Spirit
even so must the Son of Man be lifted up
For God so loved the world
whoever believes in him should not perish, but have eternal life
men loved the darkness rather than the light
He must increase, but I must decrease
He who comes from above is above all
the wrath of God remains on him

title: Samaritan woman, Samaritans believe, nobleman’s son
summary_text: Jesus leaves Judea for Galilee via Samaria, meets a Samaritan woman at Jacob’s well, offers living water, exposes her marital history, identifies as the Messiah, and many Samaritans believe and urge him to stay two days. He then teaches the disciples that his food is doing the Father’s will and that the fields are ready for harvest. In Galilee he heals a nobleman’s son at a distance—the second sign after coming from Judea.
themes: True worship in spirit and truth
key_points: Pharisees heard Jesus was making more disciples than John, so he left Judea for Galilee through Samaria.
Jesus asked a Samaritan woman for a drink, then spoke of the gift of God and living water.
He told her she had had five husbands and the man she had was not her husband.
He said worshipers must worship in spirit and truth, and identified himself as the one speaking to her.
Many Samaritans believed because of her word and then because of his word, calling him Christ and Savior of the world.
Jesus said his food was to do the will of the one who sent him and to accomplish his work.
A nobleman begged Jesus about his dying son; Jesus said “Your son lives” and the boy recovered at that hour.
The father and his whole house believed—this was the second sign after Jesus came from Judea to Galilee.
evidence_phrases: Give me a drink
living water
you have had five husbands
God is spirit
worship in spirit and truth
I am he, the one who speaks to you
He told me everything that I did
the Christ, the Savior of the world
Your son lives
This is again the second sign

title: Healing at Bethesda and the Son’s authority
summary_text: At a feast in Jerusalem, Jesus heals a man ill thirty-eight years at the pool called Bethesda and tells him to carry his mat on the Sabbath, provoking Jewish opposition. Jesus says his Father is still working and calls God his own Father, so they seek to kill him as one making himself equal with God. He then teaches that the Son does the Father’s works, gives life, has authority to judge, raises the dead, and that the Scriptures and Moses testify about him while the hearers refuse to come to him for life.
key_points: Jesus healed a long-disabled man and told him to take up his mat and walk on the Sabbath.
Jesus later warned him to sin no more so nothing worse would happen.
Jesus said his Father is still working, so he works too; Jews sought to kill him for breaking Sabbath and calling God his Father.
The Son gives life and executes judgment so that all may honor the Son as they honor the Father.
Those who hear Jesus’s word and believe him who sent him have eternal life and pass from death to life.
Jesus cites John’s witness, the Father’s witness, and his works, and says the Scriptures testify about him.
Moses accuses them, for they do not believe his writings about Jesus.
evidence_phrases: Arise, take up your mat, and walk
My Father is still working, so I am working, too
making himself equal with God
the Son also gives life to whom he desires
has passed out of death into life
resurrection of life
these are they which testify about me
if you believed Moses, you would believe me

title: Bread from heaven and hard words
summary_text: Jesus feeds about five thousand beside the Sea of Galilee, walks on the sea to the disciples’ boat, and withdraws when the crowd would make him king. In Capernaum he teaches that the work of God is to believe in him whom God sent, calls himself the bread of life come down from heaven, and speaks of eating his flesh and drinking his blood for eternal life. Many disciples turn back; Simon Peter confesses Jesus as the Holy One of God, and Jesus names Judas Iscariot as a devil who would betray him.
key_people: a boy with loaves and fish
key_points: Five barley loaves fed about five thousand men, with twelve baskets of fragments left.
Jesus walked on the sea; the boat immediately reached land.
He said the crowd sought him because they ate bread, not because they saw signs.
He declared himself the bread of life and the living bread from heaven; his flesh is given for the life of the world.
He taught that eating his flesh and drinking his blood is necessary for life and that his words are spirit and life.
Many disciples left; Peter said Jesus has the words of eternal life.
Jesus said one of the twelve is a devil—Judas, who would betray him.
evidence_phrases: about five thousand
I am the bread of life
the bread which I will give for the life of the world is my flesh
unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood
It is the spirit who gives life
You have the words of eternal life
one of you is a devil
Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot

title: Feast of Booths, teaching, and division
summary_text: Jesus stays in Galilee because Judean leaders seek to kill him, then goes secretly to Jerusalem for the Feast of Booths after his unbelieving brothers urge him to show himself openly. He teaches in the temple about his Father’s will, Moses, circumcision on the Sabbath, and his origin from the one who sent him. On the last day he cries out about living water (meaning the Spirit). The crowd divides over whether he is the Christ; officers fail to arrest him; Nicodemus urges fair hearing; the chapter ends with everyone going home.
key_points: Jesus said his time had not yet come while his brothers’ time was always ready.
He testified that the world’s works are evil and that the world hates him.
In the temple he argued from Moses and the Sabbath about making a man completely healthy.
He said they knew him and where he was from, yet did not know the Father who sent him.
On the last great day he invited the thirsty to come to him and drink rivers of living water—he spoke of the Spirit not yet given.
Many believed; chief priests and Pharisees sent officers who returned saying no one spoke like Jesus.
Nicodemus asked whether the law judges without first hearing the accused.
evidence_phrases: his brothers didn’t believe in him
My teaching is not mine, but his who sent me
If anyone desires to do his will, he will know about the teaching
If anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink
the Spirit, which those believing in him were to receive
No man ever spoke like this man!
Does our law judge a man, unless it first hears from him
Everyone went to his own house

title: Adultery case, light, Abraham, and I AM
summary_text: Scribes and Pharisees bring a woman caught in adultery; Jesus tells whoever is without sin to cast the first stone, and they leave; he sends her away without condemning her, telling her to sin no more. He declares himself the light of the world, disputes his testimony and judgment with the Pharisees, warns that without believing “I am he” they will die in their sins, and debates true sonship of Abraham versus doing the devil’s works. After claiming existence before Abraham, they take up stones but Jesus passes through them.
key_points: Jesus invited the sinless to throw the first stone; accusers departed from oldest to youngest.
He told the woman he did not condemn her and to sin no more.
He said he is the light of the world; followers will not walk in darkness.
He promised that knowing the truth would make them free, and that the Son makes them free indeed.
He charged his hearers with seeking to kill him and attributed their unbelief to being of their father the devil.
He said Abraham rejoiced to see his day.
He said, “before Abraham came into existence, I AM,” and they tried to stone him.
evidence_phrases: He who is without sin among you, let him throw the first stone at her
Neither do I condemn you
I am the light of the world
the truth will make you free
You are of your father, the devil
before Abraham came into existence, I AM
they took up stones to throw at him
went out of the temple, having gone through the middle of them

title: Man born blind: sight, conflict, and worship
summary_text: Jesus heals a man blind from birth by sending him to wash mud from his eyes at Siloam, saying God’s works should be displayed while it is day and that he is the light of the world. Pharisees dispute whether the healer is from God because of the Sabbath; the man’s parents fear the synagogue rulers; the healed man reasons from his experience and is cast out. Jesus finds him, reveals himself as the Son of God, receives his worship, and teaches that judgment turns on spiritual blindness versus sight.
key_points: Neither the man nor his parents sinned to cause his blindness; it was so God’s works might be revealed.
Jesus sent him to wash in the pool of Siloam and he returned seeing.
Pharisees were divided over whether Jesus was from God.
His parents confirmed he was their blind son but would not say how he saw, fearing expulsion from the synagogue.
The man insisted that God hears those who worship him and do his will.
Jesus asked if he believed in the Son of God and revealed he was the one speaking with him; the man believed and worshiped.
Jesus said those who say they see remain in sin.
evidence_phrases: that the works of God might be revealed in him
Go, wash in the pool of Siloam
I am the light of the world
though I was blind, now I see
If this man were not from God, he could do nothing
Do you believe in the Son of God?
Lord, I believe!
your sin remains

title: Good shepherd, one with the Father, beyond Jordan
summary_text: Jesus teaches that he is the door and the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep, knows his own, has other sheep to gather into one flock, and takes his life again by the Father’s command. At the Feast of Dedication in Solomon’s porch, he says his works testify and that he and the Father are one; Jews again try to stone and seize him. He escapes to where John first baptized, and many believe because John’s words about him were true.
themes: I and the Father are one
key_points: The shepherd enters by the door; sheep know his voice and flee strangers.
Jesus said he is the door by which one is saved and finds pasture.
The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep, unlike a hired hand who flees.
He has other sheep not of this fold to bring so there is one flock and one shepherd.
He claimed power to lay down his life and take it again as the Father commanded.
He said, “I and the Father are one,” and Jews accused him of blasphemy for making himself God.
Many believed beyond the Jordan where John had baptized.
evidence_phrases: I am the good shepherd
I came that they may have life, and may have it abundantly
one flock with one shepherd
I lay it down by myself
I and the Father are one
you, being a man, make yourself God
everything that John said about this man is true
Many believed in him there

title: Lazarus raised and a plot to kill Jesus
summary_text: Lazarus of Bethany falls sick and dies while Jesus delays two days; Jesus tells the disciples Lazarus is dead and goes to Judea despite danger. Martha and Mary meet him; he declares himself the resurrection and the life, weeps at the tomb, and calls Lazarus out alive after four days. Many Jews believe, but the council fears Rome and Caiaphas says one man should die for the people—a saying interpreted as prophetic about Jesus dying for the nation and gathering God’s scattered children. Leaders resolve to kill Jesus; he withdraws to Ephraim as Passover nears.
key_points: Jesus said Lazarus’s sickness was not to death but for God’s Son to be glorified.
He told Martha he is the resurrection and the life; she confessed him as Christ, God’s Son.
Jesus wept, prayed aloud to the Father, and cried, “Lazarus, come out!”
The dead man came out bound; Jesus told them to loose him.
The council feared all would believe and Rome would destroy their place and nation.
Caiaphas advised that one man die for the people rather than the whole nation perish.
From that day they plotted to put Jesus to death; he stayed in Ephraim with disciples.
evidence_phrases: This sickness is not to death, but for the glory of God
I am the resurrection and the life
Jesus wept
Lazarus, come out!
everyone will believe in him, and the Romans will come
it is advantageous for us that one man should die for the people
Jesus would die for the nation
they took counsel that they might put him to death

title: Anointing, entry, Greeks, and unbelief
summary_text: Six days before Passover at Bethany, Mary anoints Jesus’s feet with costly nard; Judas Iscariot objects while stealing from the money box. A crowd comes for Jesus and raised Lazarus; chief priests plot to kill Lazarus too. The next day Jesus enters Jerusalem on a donkey’s colt amid Hosannas; Greeks wish to see him through Philip and Andrew. Jesus speaks of the hour of the Son of Man’s glorification, the grain of wheat dying, service, a voice from heaven, judgment of this world, and being lifted up. Many still do not believe, fulfilling Isaiah; rulers believe secretly; Jesus urges belief in light and says his words are the Father’s command for eternal life.
themes: Prince of this world cast out
key_points: Mary anointed Jesus’s feet; Judas complained though he was a thief.
Chief priests planned to kill Lazarus because many Jews believed on account of him.
Crowds hailed Jesus with palm branches as King of Israel on a donkey’s colt.
Greeks asked Philip to see Jesus; Jesus said the hour had come for the Son of Man to be glorified.
A voice from heaven answered his prayer to glorify the Father’s name.
He said he would draw all people to himself when lifted up, signifying his death.
Isaiah’s words about blinded eyes and hardened hearts were fulfilled in their unbelief.
Many rulers believed secretly for fear of the Pharisees.
evidence_phrases: She has kept this for the day of my burial
he was a thief, and having the money box
Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord
Sir, we want to see Jesus
The time has come for the Son of Man to be glorified
I have both glorified it, and will glorify it again
I, if I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself
they loved men’s praise more than God’s praise

title: Foot washing, betrayal, and love one another
summary_text: Before Passover, knowing his hour has come, Jesus washes the disciples’ feet as an example of humble service, telling Peter that without this washing one has no part with him and that not all are clean. He identifies the betrayer by giving dipped bread to Judas, into whom Satan then enters; Judas leaves at night. Jesus speaks of glorification, his impending departure, a new commandment to love as he loved, and predicts Peter will deny him three times before the rooster crows.
key_points: Jesus washed the disciples’ feet and told them to wash one another’s feet as he had done.
He said one who is bathed needs only feet washed; Judas was not clean.
He quoted Scripture about the one who ate bread lifting his heel against him.
He gave dipped bread to Judas; after it Satan entered Judas, who went out into the night.
Jesus gave a new commandment: love one another as he loved them.
By this love all would know they are his disciples.
He told Peter the rooster would not crow until Peter had denied him three times.
evidence_phrases: he loved them to the end
If I don’t wash you, you have no part with me
You are clean, but not all of you
Satan entered into him
What you do, do quickly
A new commandment I give to you
if you have love for one another
the rooster won’t crow until you have denied me three times

title: Way, truth, life, and the Counselor
summary_text: Jesus tells troubled disciples to believe in God and in him, promising many dwelling places in the Father’s house and his return to receive them. He is the way, truth, and life; seeing him is seeing the Father. He promises greater works, prayer in his name, another Counselor—the Spirit of truth—love shown by keeping commandments, the Father and Son making their home with the lover, peace unlike the world’s, and obedience though the prince of the world comes with no claim on him.
themes: Peace and the prince of the world
key_points: Jesus goes to prepare a place and will come again to take disciples to himself.
He said no one comes to the Father except through him.
Whoever has seen him has seen the Father; the Father is in him and he in the Father.
Believers will do greater works because he goes to the Father; whatever is asked in his name he will do.
The Father will send another Counselor, the Spirit of truth, to teach and remind them.
Jesus leaves peace, not as the world gives.
The prince of the world comes but has nothing in Jesus.
evidence_phrases: In my Father’s house are many homes
I am the way, the truth, and the life
He who has seen me has seen the Father
he will do greater works than these
another Counselor
the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name
Peace I leave with you
the prince of the world comes, and he has nothing in me

title: Vine, love, hatred, and the Spirit’s witness
summary_text: Jesus teaches that he is the true vine: branches must remain in him to bear fruit; unfruitful branches are removed and burned. Joy is full as disciples keep his commandments, especially loving one another as he loved them, with the greatest love being laying down one’s life. The world hates them as it hated him; sin is inexcusable after his words and works. The Counselor from the Father will testify about him, and the disciples will testify because they have been with him from the beginning.
key_points: The Father prunes branches so they bear more fruit; apart from Jesus one can do nothing.
Branches not remaining in him are burned.
Keeping Jesus’s commandments and remaining in his love fills joy.
His command is to love one another as he loved them; friends obey and know what he heard from the Father.
The world hates them because he chose them out of the world.
They have no excuse for sin after his coming and signs.
The Counselor, Spirit of truth, will testify about Jesus; disciples will testify too.
evidence_phrases: I am the true vine
apart from me you can do nothing
If a man doesn’t remain in me, he is thrown out as a branch
that my joy may remain in you
Greater love has no one than this
You didn’t choose me, but I chose you
They hated me without a cause
he will testify about me

title: Sorrow, Counselor, and overcoming the world
summary_text: Jesus warns of expulsion from synagogues and deadly hatred done in God’s name out of ignorance of the Father and him. It is better that he go so the Counselor may come to convict the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment. The Spirit will guide into all truth and glorify Jesus. Disciples grieve his departure but will rejoice when they see him again; the Father will give what they ask in Jesus’s name. Jesus speaks plainly of going to the Father; they profess belief, yet he foretells scattering and leaves peace and courage—they have trouble in the world, but he has overcome the world.
key_points: Enemies will think killing Jesus’s followers is serving God.
The Counselor convicts about sin (unbelief), righteousness (Jesus to the Father), and judgment (prince of this world judged).
The Spirit will guide into all truth and declare what is coming.
Sorrow will turn to joy like a woman after childbirth.
The Father loves them and will give what they ask in Jesus’s name.
Jesus came from the Father and returns to the Father.
Disciples will be scattered and leave him alone, yet the Father is with him.
In him they may have peace; he has overcome the world.
evidence_phrases: whoever kills you will think that he offers service to God
It is to your advantage that I go away
he will convict the world about sin, about righteousness, and about judgment
he will guide you into all truth
your sorrow will be turned into joy
whatever you may ask of the Father in my name, he will give it to you
you will be scattered, everyone to his own place
I have overcome the world

title: Jesus prays for disciples and future believers
summary_text: Jesus prays aloud to the Father to glorify the Son so the Son may glorify the Father, defining eternal life as knowing the only true God and Jesus Christ whom he sent. He prays for those the Father gave him: they kept his word, are not of the world, need keeping from the evil one, and sanctification in truth. He prays for their unity as the Father and Son are one, that the world may believe, and for future believers through the apostles’ word. He asks that they see his glory and that the Father’s love be in them as he is in them.
key_points: Eternal life is knowing the only true God and him whom he sent, Jesus Christ.
Jesus glorified the Father on earth and asks to be glorified with pre-world glory.
He prays for those given him, not the world, that they be kept in the Father’s name.
None was lost except the son of destruction, to fulfill Scripture.
He sanctifies himself for their sake that they may be sanctified in truth.
He prays future believers may be one so the world may believe God sent him.
He desires they be with him to see his glory and that the Father’s love dwell in them.
evidence_phrases: Glorify your Son, that your Son may also glorify you
This is eternal life, that they should know you, the only true God, and him whom you sent, Jesus Christ
I pray for them. I don’t pray for the world
None of them is lost except the son of destruction
Sanctify them in your truth. Your word is truth
that they may all be one
that the world may know that you sent me and loved them
that the love with which you loved me may be in them, and I in them

title: Arrest, denials, and trial before Pilate
summary_text: After his prayer, Jesus crosses the Kidron to a garden; Judas leads soldiers and officers who arrest him after he twice identifies himself as “I am he,” and the disciples are let go. Peter cuts off Malchus’s ear; Jesus is bound and questioned first before Annas, then sent to Caiaphas, while Peter denies Jesus three times by a charcoal fire and the rooster crows. Jesus is taken to Pilate; Jews refuse to enter the Praetorium; Pilate finds no charge; they demand Barabbas instead of the King of the Jews.
key_people: a maid who kept the door
key_points: Judas brought a detachment with lanterns and weapons to the garden Jesus often used.
Jesus identified himself; they drew back and fell; he secured the disciples’ release.
Peter struck Malchus; Jesus told him to sheath the sword and spoke of drinking the Father’s cup.
Jesus was sent from Annas to Caiaphas; an officer struck him for answering the high priest.
Peter denied being a disciple three times; the rooster crowed immediately.
Pilate found no basis for a charge; Jews said they could not put anyone to death.
Jesus said his kingdom is not of this world and that he was born to testify to the truth.
The crowd chose Barabbas over Jesus.
evidence_phrases: I am he
let these go their way
Put the sword into its sheath
The cup which the Father has given me
Annas sent him bound to Caiaphas
Peter therefore denied it again, and immediately the rooster crowed
My Kingdom is not of this world
Not this man, but Barabbas!

title: Crucifixion, death, and burial
summary_text: Pilate has Jesus flogged; soldiers mock him with a crown of thorns and purple robe. Despite Pilate’s reluctance, chief priests insist he must die for making himself God’s Son; Pilate yields to Caesar-threats, delivers Jesus, and posts the title “Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews.” Jesus is crucified between two others at Golgotha; he commits his mother to the beloved disciple, says it is finished, and gives up his spirit. Soldiers break the others’ legs but pierce Jesus’s side; Scripture is cited. Joseph of Arimathaea and Nicodemus bury him in a new nearby tomb before the Preparation Day ends.
key_points: Pilate showed Jesus crowned and robed; crowds cried crucify though Pilate found no guilt.
Jews cited their law: he made himself the Son of God.
Pilate wrote the cross title in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek and would not change it.
Jesus gave his mother to the disciple and the disciple to her.
He said “I am thirsty,” received vinegar, said “It is finished,” and died.
His legs were not broken; a soldier pierced his side; blood and water came out.
Joseph asked Pilate for the body; Nicodemus brought a great weight of spices.
They buried Jesus in a new tomb in a garden because the tomb was near.
evidence_phrases: Behold, the man!
he made himself the Son of God
JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS
Woman, behold, your son!
It is finished
they didn’t break his legs
blood and water came out
bound it in linen cloths with the spices

title: Empty tomb, Mary, and Thomas
summary_text: Mary Magdalene finds the stone rolled away and tells Peter and the beloved disciple, who see empty linen cloths and believe though they yet lack understanding of resurrection Scripture. Mary meets Jesus, who sends her to tell the brothers he ascends to his Father and God. That evening Jesus appears to fearful, locked-in disciples, shows his wounds, commissions them as the Father sent him, and breathes the Holy Spirit with authority about forgiveness. Thomas doubts until eight days later he touches Jesus’s wounds and confesses him as Lord and God; Jesus blesses those who believe without seeing and states the book’s purpose: belief in Christ the Son of God for life in his name.
key_points: The tomb was open; Peter and the other disciple saw linen wrappings and the face cloth rolled apart.
Mary mistook Jesus for the gardener until he called her name.
Jesus told her not to hold him and to announce his ascent to his Father and their Father.
He appeared through locked doors, showed hands and side, and said peace and sending as the Father sent him.
He breathed on them, saying receive the Holy Spirit, with words about retaining and forgiving sins.
Thomas required seeing nail prints and the side; Jesus invited him to see and believe.
Thomas called him “My Lord and my God!”
These things are written that readers may believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and have life in his name.
evidence_phrases: he saw and believed
Rabboni!
I am ascending to my Father and your Father
Peace be to you
As the Father has sent me, even so I send you
Receive the Holy Spirit!
Don’t be unbelieving, but believing
that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God

title: Fish, breakfast, and Peter restored
summary_text: Jesus appears by the Sea of Tiberias to Peter, Thomas, Nathanael, Zebedee’s sons, and two others; after a fruitless night he directs a catch of 153 large fish and serves bread and fish on shore—his third post-resurrection revelation. He three times asks Simon son of Jonah whether Simon loves him, commissioning him to feed lambs and sheep, and foretells Peter’s bound hands and death glorifying God, then says “Follow me.” Peter asks about the beloved disciple; Jesus replies it is not Peter’s concern if that one should remain until Jesus comes—a rumor misunderstood as immortality. The beloved disciple attests that his written witness is true.
key_points: They caught nothing until Jesus told them to cast the net on the right; the net held 153 large fish without tearing.
Jesus had fish and bread on a charcoal fire and invited them to breakfast.
This was the third time Jesus showed himself to the disciples after rising.
Jesus asked Peter three times about love and commanded feed my lambs, tend my sheep, feed my sheep.
He foretold Peter would be carried where he did not wish, signifying the death by which Peter would glorify God.
Jesus told Peter not to concern himself with whether the beloved disciple remains until Jesus comes.
The beloved disciple is identified as the one who testifies and wrote these things.

one hundred fifty-three great fish
Come and eat breakfast!
Do you love me more than these?
Feed my lambs
Tend my sheep
Follow me
If I desire that he stay until I come, what is that to you?
